---
title: "Choosing families for ecological responses"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Choosing families for ecological responses}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>")
```

This article will help users choose response families for biological and
environmental response data: continuous traits, counts, proportions,
percentages, positive measurements, shape-sensitive traits, and ordinal scores.

The current planning reference is `docs/design/06-distribution-roadmap.md`.

Meta-analysis is not listed as a separate family. It uses `family = gaussian()`
with `meta_known_V(V = V)`.

For bivariate models, the preferred public direction is to combine response
families:

```r
family = c(gaussian(), gaussian())
family = c(gaussian(), poisson())
```

This keeps mixed-response bivariate models natural for ecological data, for
example body mass with fecundity counts, survival with dispersal counts, or leaf
area with seed number. The current `biv_gaussian()` object is a prototype for
the all-Gaussian case, not a commitment to one named family per response
combination.
